title: A Mindfulness Guide for the Frazzled
author: Jem Lerner

chapters.title: Information overload has left us in a state of constant agitation
chapters.text:
Most people are exhausted nowadays — they suffer extreme physical and mental fatigue. Instead of living in the present, they worry about the future and fixate on past mistakes. Individuals often compare their lives to that of others, trying to keep up with the Joneses. They move from task to task and even attempt to do two or three things simultaneously, which leaves them burned out. Continual stress triggers the nervous system, overflowing it with adrenaline and cortisol.

Despite living in one of the most successful and peaceful eras in human history, people constantly create stress where there is none.

Although people have achieved significant technological advancements, they often neglect the importance of emotional well-being. Why don't humans appreciate that, unlike their primitive ancestors, they can live long lives without always watching their backs for predators? Is it worth constantly competing with others for societal status and recognition?

Despite numerous distractions, an average individual produces around 100,000 words daily while communicating with friends. This information overload comes at a cost; finding out what people need is exhausting. It’s high time humans got themselves out of this rut of constant agitation and started cultivating mindfulness.

Stay tuned to try the renowned six-week mindfulness training program to break the pattern of unhealthy behavior. Each week has specific exercises to help you learn the ‘how-to’ of mindfulness. As a result, your mind calms down, and you can enjoy your life without wandering in your thoughts. Once you know how to stay present, you regain control over your existence.

Did you know? According to research by the US National Institute of Health, meditation can extend life expectancy.

chapters.title: Control your attention to calm your mind
chapters.text:
Mindfulness isn’t related to spirituality or magic — it means practicing focus-switching. It’s a way to quiet the critical thoughts in your head by shifting attention to something on the inside.

My definition of mindfulness is noticing your thoughts and feelings without kicking your own ass while you’re doing it. ~ Ruby Wax

Look closely at your thoughts; you can take away their power and realize they don’t belong to you. Slowly inhale, relax, and watch your thoughts float around. When you stop trying to hold onto them, they fade away.

To illustrate this process, imagine a clear glass of water with sand on the bottom; once you start shaking it, the liquid becomes muddy. Likewise, your thoughts can harm your state of mind when you react to them. The sand settles when you hold the bottle still. Try observing rather than responding.

Perceiving things as they are is a fundamental aspect of mindfulness. You may encounter something pleasant, unkind, or disgraceful, but don't overthink it. It might be hard to quiet down the instinct initially, but you'll improve with practice.

As with any skill, mastering the art of mindfulness requires constant practice. Time after time, the part of your brain responsible for attention slowly learns a new pattern. Regaining control will be challenging; the human mind reacts to every trigger, which is crucial for survival.

Proper attention control is essential to living a better, happier, healthier life. It’s more efficient to have power over your brain instead of being run ragged by it. Remember that you do not have to achieve complete mindfulness — being in this state might interfere with your everyday life.

Attention is like a muscle — the more you use it, the more potent it becomes.

Once you’ve learned to control your attention, you can make the right decisions, even amid everyday life's emotional turmoil.

Humans are multifaceted; they never know which aspect of their personalities will surface. You may be shy when an attractive stranger smiles at you in a restaurant and then feel frustrated when your colleague crosses the line. Decluttering your mind allows you to access various aspects of yourself and choose the most appropriate one based on the situation.

chapters.title: Work on switching off your inner autopilot
chapters.text:
People like to measure success by comparing it to others. Unlike your other achievements, the purpose of mindfulness has nothing to do with getting validation from a paternal figure, your boss, friends, or your partner. Here, you are working on a better mindset for yourself. Also, remember that you don’t have to flip your life upside down. Aim to implement exercises into your lifestyle.

Now that you know all the advantages of paying attention, it's time to consider how to execute it. Start by getting a journal to write your reflections. It's beneficial for your progress to record your thoughts regularly.

Presence is an experience you may not comprehend logically; you can only acknowledge it through your senses.

The first week of the mindfulness training course is about noticing and waking up. The main exercise for this week is “taste.” Here is how you go about it:

Find something you like to eat, such as chocolate, cake, fruit, or meatballs, and cut it into bite-sized pieces. Place a small amount into the palm of your hand. Now, focus on it as if you haven’t seen it before. Pay attention to the edges, shape, and color. Gradually, experience the internal feelings as you grab food in your hand from the table and then put it in your mouth.

Notice the morsel's weight, shape, and taste in your mouth. Wait for a few moments before chewing, carefully exploring the taste, and paying attention to its structure and flavor.

Use these questions to guide yourself:

• Was this experience different from your usual eating habits? In what way?
• Was there something new that you noticed about your favorite food?
• Was there a moment when you switched your focus to something else? What was that?

Write down your reflections in your journal.

To incorporate this exercise into your routine, choose regular tasks or habits, such as brushing your teeth or having breakfast. While preparing tea, savor each moment, from filling the cup and smelling the herbs to finally tasting it. Try to be fully present and aware of each sensation, even if only for a brief moment.

chapters.title: Breathing techniques help reduce stress
chapters.text:
As discussed, the first week's exercise aims to help you notice the difference between your old habits and your new approach. Seeing these details allows you to turn off the autopilot momentarily. Next week, you will learn to focus on your anchors — sound and breath.

Intentionality births mindfulness — try to return to the present moment.

Before starting, you should not shame yourself for doing it wrong. Sometimes, people are too harsh on themselves. This week, aim to discover that you can switch your focus.

When ready for practice, sit in your chair with your spine as straight as possible and maintain a relaxed posture. Try to sense how you touch the ground with your feet. Next, move your attention to the parts of the body that feel the chair. Finally, complete the scanning process of your sensations.

Now, shift your attention to the various sounds in your environment, regardless of their volume or source. In a minute or two, your mind will attempt to evaluate what it can hear, stirring up some emotions. When this happens, notice and gently guide your attention back to the sounds.

After a short pause, pay attention to your breathing. Travel around your body and analyze each part — your nose, chest, and belly. The sensations differ — your chest rises, while your stomach expands when you breathe. You can feel the air’s movement in your nose — cold when you inhale and warm when you exhale. The more details you can notice, the more effective the exercise becomes.

After you finish, you can reflect on the new experience by answering the question in your journal:

• Was your experience different from your usual breathing and listening?
• Was it challenging for you to stay focused?
• Did you struggle with fixing your attention? In what moments have you lost focus?

Implement this exercise in your daily routine, whether in the morning or the evening. Use a timer to ensure you spend equal time on each part. You will notice that it becomes easier for you to hold your focus on one thing. Besides, breathing techniques help you to compose yourself before final exams or public speaking.

chapters.title: Reconnect your body and mind through practice
chapters.text:
Even though your brain is in your head, it connects with each cell, hair, and nail. The third week’s exercise is about mindful movement. It aims to help you become more conscious about your body and understand that your relationship with it results from your thoughts.

Your body shows your inner self, not who you think you are or who you wish to be.

You can reach more outstanding results by implementing mindfulness into your physical activities. Moreover, once you are mentally connected to your body, you start noticing every inch of it, so you can immediately sense any tension or resistance. Individuals mistakenly believe they improve their bodies by suffering in a gym. It may look like that, but your physical weaknesses indicate the issues you have in your mind. So, by recognizing the signals, you can solve much more significant problems than a flat butt.

During the third week, you can start building a new relationship with your body by practicing a new exercise — stretching your sides.

Stretch your arms above your head. Pay attention to the feelings in your body while making each movement. Is it difficult to hold the weight of your hands? Then, slowly start to bend to the left side of your waist, moving your body. Feel the limits, and don’t go beyond your comfort zone. Enjoy a nice relaxing stretch without any pain.

With your arms above your head, return upright and lean your body to the right. Now, you should feel the tension in the right part of the body and release it on the left. Move back to the initial pose. Relax and slowly put your arms down. Practice this exercise four times every day, two on each side.

These movements help hold on to your body as an anchor. After some time, you will find relief in this activity. Note that you can return to it in case you experience mental hardships. Stretching helps to relax the muscles. But when you engage in physical activities, your body and mind free up. Practice this exercise for 10-20 minutes daily for six days.

The more physical activities you perform, the more oxygen the bloodstream will deliver to your brain. Remember, a rigid body is a stiff mind.

chapters.title: Get in tune with your emotional self
chapters.text:
Now that you’ve practiced focusing on sounds, body, and breathing, it’s time to pay attention to where you feel emotions. The primary aim is to get comfortable moving toward your feelings rather than away from them. People choose the avoidance behavior pattern over starting the healing process because of fear. However, you can’t run away from yourself; your repressed emotions can cause long-term damage.

Cultivating pure awareness of your emotions takes you beyond negative self-talk.

The sooner you figure out the harmful feelings that poison you from the inside, the faster you can find a way out of it. It’s easier to deal with new emotions before you reach the stage of self-shaming and intrusive negative thinking. When you control the situation, you can prevent the consequences. The next step is to recognize the strategies you implement subconsciously. It requires deep self-awareness to notice them and change.

Imagine you have some physical trauma that occasionally bothers you. For instance, your knee starts to ache when you squat. The pain you've been experiencing has become a part of your self-identity for so long that you don't attempt to change it. The same can occur with emotional traumas you carry for years, as they are part of you. Let’s discover techniques that can reconnect you with your inner world.

The Mindful Emotions exercise is excellent for fostering acknowledgment of your feelings. Sit down comfortably and melt into the chair. First, focus on your feet touching the ground. In a moment, let it go and move your attention to your new focal point. The key is not to force it. Allow the breath to happen on its own. You can mentally count to ten breaths if it helps.

Now, look at your body from the inside and try to sense what’s there. Some parts of it will get your attention. When it happens, concentrate on it as if it’s under a microscope. Remember, you don’t need to come up with an answer. Please focus on the emotion you have found and feel it. What physical sensation does it give you? Repeat it daily, and keep noting your impressions in a journal.

chapters.title: Check on your thoughts regularly
chapters.text:
The fifth week of the course is about observing your thoughts with greater clarity and settled, mindful thinking. Over the past few weeks, you have been learning to distance yourself from all the worries and look at them as you stand a few steps away.

This week, you will play the role of your inner shrink. Listen to your deep, dark thoughts without criticism, like a therapist who brings no judgment.

The human mind can only reveal its true self when it feels safe and secure.

Once you realize who you are, you can unchain yourself from any limiting beliefs and thoughts you might have discovered and replace them with new ones. Otherwise, you risk getting stuck in your mental trap, where you follow the same irrelevant behavioral patterns for years. Periods of self-immersion stimulate your creativity, bring new perspectives you didn't notice before, or even lead to breakthrough moments. You might finally learn to distinguish which thoughts are valuable and which damage your perception of the world.

Let's do the mindful thinking exercise. Start in the same position on a chair, focusing on your feet. Then, switch your attention to your breathing and surrounding sounds. Don’t look for the sound; listen to the outer world and let it come.

Focus on your thoughts; let them pop up into your head without fixation on any particular one. You can imagine them as clouds in the sky — all are different shapes, sizes, and colors. They move around separately, and you don’t need to interact with them.

Another idea is to imagine yourself watching a movie with familiar actors in the cinema. You’re sitting and enjoying it without comments or judgment. The film can be dramatic, scary, or you may have a comedy on your mind. Sit a short distance from what’s on the screen. If you start self-talking or overthinking the events, you must switch focus back to your present moment.

At the end of the exercise, come back to breathing.

The breath is always there for you to bring you back to a sense of peace and presence. ~ Ruby Wax

Remember to write down your reflections in a journal after each practice.

chapters.title: How to implement new exercises to your schedule
chapters.text:
The sixth and final week will work on integrating mindfulness into your everyday life. The truth is, it’s impossible to stay mindful 24/7, especially if you aren’t a Tibetan monk. Sometimes you have to be on autopilot. Let’s check your daily schedule to find gaps and implement exercises that can benefit your state of mind:

The day of an ordinary employed person usually looks the same:

7 AM: Try waking up slightly earlier for a five-to-ten-minute mindfulness session. You can do any of the exercises discussed earlier. Be mindful while showering or brushing your teeth if you have no spare time. Vivid dreams and nightmares leave a mark on your mind — you might think about them all day. With mindful thinking, you dispose of these odd sensations.

8 AM: While having breakfast, you can mindfully explore your food. Feel your breakfast meal's temperature, flavor, size, or taste. Don’t rush. It’s crucial to slow down and start your day smoothly.

8:30 AM: If you drive to work, this is the perfect time to be on autopilot. Don’t try to focus on your senses while moving. If you are using public transport, make the most of this time. You can shift your attention to the feelings inside your body, sitting on your seat and focusing on your breath.

9 AM – 1 PM: If you notice yourself getting a brain clog at any time in the morning, do a two- to three-minute mindfulness practice at your workplace.

With a clear mind, you will be more creative and energized and have an incredibly productive day.

1 PM: Lunchtime. What about having one more meal, mindfully enjoying every bit?

7 PM: When you finish your work, you must clear your mind and leave your work-related thoughts behind. Before returning home, do a short mindfulness exercise.

11 PM: Before bed, a three-minute exercise can help you to relax. Focus on your breath as you drift to sleep.

As you can see, this plan is only a draft you can edit and use for your lifestyle. Stick to a new habit to see the results of a mindful life.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
In today's world, people are bombarded with tons of information daily. The cost of this lifestyle is high. Individuals need help filtering essential things from unnecessary ones. Their brains are so overloaded that it becomes difficult to think clearly.

Unlike your phone, the human brain doesn’t need charging; however, it requires rest periods. For most people of the current generation, relaxation is a luxury they cannot afford. Rather than get adequate rest, humans pump themselves full of caffeine daily and overwork. This practice is why most people phase through life without experiencing the joys of it.

Mindfulness and heightened awareness are key to a happy, healthy, and fulfilled life. The ability to turn off the autopilot and immerse yourself in a moment takes practice, but this approach eventually gives you tranquility and peace. When you are self-aware, you accept things the way they are. Instead of worrying about every thought in your head, you observe them without judgment and let them come and go as they please. And by being a passive spectator of your thoughts and feelings, you gain total control of your attention and start to live fully in the here and now.

Try this



• Practice all the mindfulness exercises highlighted in this summary regularly and incorporate them into your daily activities.
• Whenever you need to improve your mental state, use focus to come back to the present moment.
• Remember, we can only truly experience life by being present in the moment, not by contemplating the future or reminiscing.

Read it till the end?
